content.full_text:
CITY OF CARLSBAD
1200 CARLSBAD VILLAGE DRIVE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-1989
(619) 434-2891

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
CITY ATTORNEY

RONALD R. BALL
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

January 24, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: I-89-646 - REQUEST FOR SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION AND
CLARIFICATION

Thank you for your letter of January 3, 1990 and the above
referenced informal assistance. However, it did not provide the
guidance I requested. (It was also addressed to City Attorney
Biondi (sic) making me wonder how that can be since the name is
clearly printed on the letterhead.)

Your letter concludes that I may participate in the SDG&E power
plant proposed for Carlsbad unless that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Edison. That doesn't
answer the question. Will my activities in Carlsbad, as the City
pursues its efforts to stop construction of the plant, have that
effect and how am I supposed to determine it? The status quo is
no power plant. The ultimate decision about whether or not one is
to be constructed will be made by the Energy Commission, the Public
Utilities Commission and the Coastal Commission. If all three
state agencies approve the plant it is my understanding that it can
be constructed notwithstanding Carlsbad's opposition. If we
succeed in persuading any of those state agencies to disapprove the
plant then the status quo is maintained. How can that have a
material financial effect on SDG&E? If we are unsuccessful and the
plant is approved by the state agencies it may be constructed in
spite of our efforts so how can our failure have a material
financial effect on SDG&E?

I would also appreciate guidance on the foreseeability discussion
which concludes: "... that there is a substantial likelihood that
the merger will occur, . . ." I don't think the power plant decision
in Carlsbad will have a material financial effect on SDG&E since,
if they don't build it here they will build it someplace else. It
is even less clear to me how that effect can be attributed to
Southern California Edison. However, since your opinion appears
to turn on the question of whether or not the merger will go
through, I have asked the City Attorney's office of San Diego its
opinion on that matter. Mr. C. M. Fitzpatrick, Assistant City


Attorney, who is in charge of the City of San Diego's opposition to the merger, reports in a letter dated January 22, 1990 that, "I obviously do not personally agree that there is a substantial likelihood that the proposed merger will occur." He reports that the merger will not be approved by the Public Utilities Commission unless the companies carry their burden of proof that the proposal is in the best interests of the rate payers of both companies. He reports further that the City of San Diego has submitted voluminous expert testimony before the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission clearly showing, in the City's view, that the monetary savings which the companies claim will occur as a result of the proposed merger are, "...speculative, overstated and illusory." Mr. Fitzpatrick reports further, "... our experts advise that the proposal is violative on its face of the federal antitrust laws and have so testified before the FERC in documentary form." Mr. Fitzpatrick concludes that the analysis by Jeevan S. Ahuja of your legal division that there is a "substantial" likelihood is "at least suspect." I agree. I think at this point it is impossible to say whether or not the merger will occur. It seems to me that before your agency disqualifies the City's attorney and thereby requires the City Council to retain expensive outside counsel, there should be more to the potential conflict then mere speculation.

I also would appreciate additional guidance on the materiality question. I understand what the guidelines say. Southern California Edison is a multibillion dollar company and, if the merger is approved will become the nation's largest utility. Whether or not one small additional power plant is located in Carlsbad or elsewhere in its service territory doesn't seem to me to involve a material financial effect on a company of that size. Utility rate making is an extremely complex matter and I am at a loss to understand how I can possibly determine whether or not if the power plant is located outside Carlsbad, that will result in a material financial effect on Edison.

We need additional guidance. Unless you are able to conclude that the City Attorney may participate my city will be forced to retain outside counsel. Before publicly making that recommendation, I need to be satisfied that the Fair Political Practices Commission is giving our city council no alternative.

Your prompt attention to this supplemental request for assistance would be appreciated.

Very truly yours,

VINCENT F. BIGGIO, JR.
City Attorney

rmh

I apologize, but the image provided is extremely faint and largely illegible. The document appears to be a multi-page text document, but the content is too washed out for me to accurately transcribe the text verbatim. 

To provide you with an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer, higher-contrast version of this document. Could you please provide a better quality image?
